title: Offices of Physical, Occupational and Speech Therapists, and Audiologists

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 621340 ($25,528,793,330) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 621340 ($45,314,241,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1580 (margins.json[62]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Hospitals/Healthcare Facilities EBITDA/Sales 15.80% (closest named bucket for provider economics; public support-services bucket at 3.87% reflects a different business model)); revenue/employee $97,549 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 621340); class <5: 19,498 firms, avg revenue/firm $219,208 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $34,635 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 5,169 firms, avg revenue/firm $664,181 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $104,941 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 3,624 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,193,863 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $188,630 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 2,575 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,111,227 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $491,574 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 460 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,045,304 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,061,158 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 361 firms, avg revenue/firm $53,374,413 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $8,433,157 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 821
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Hospitals/Healthcare Facilities EBITDA/Sales 15.80% (closest named bucket for provider economics; public support-services bucket at 3.87% reflects a different business model)

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Licensed hands-on care billed as timed CPT units — therapist minutes ARE the revenue unit, so labor substitution destroys revenue (billing-unit trap). Only the documentation sliver is AI-touchable: AI scribes reclaim unbilled note-writing time (WebPT 2024 SORT: 85% of clinicians document at home), which converts to throughput, not headcount cuts. 5% reflects the cost-side value of that reclaimed time.
Same structure as PT: licensed clinical time is the billed product. Teletherapy shifted delivery online but it is still human-delivered. Documentation sliver only.
Licensed hands-on care, timed-code billing; documentation sliver only.
Licensed/certified hands-on extenders whose modifier-flagged services Medicare pays at 85% — a reimbursement policy fact, not an AI surface. Near-zero automatable content beyond minor note-drafting.
Room setup, equipment turnover, scheduling support — partially absorbable via AI scheduling optimization and self-serve check-in; the physical setup work remains.

Scheduling, eligibility verification, prior-authorization paperwork, claims and denials — the clean AI-automatable base. Current agentic/RCM AI handles a majority of these workflows; in-person reception and complex denial appeals persist.
Compliance reporting and revenue-cycle oversight automate; referral-relationship management and people management persist.

breakdown_by_role.role: Remainder / long tail (audiologists, athletic trainers, exercise physiologists, billing tail, marketing, techs)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Mixed clinical-adjacent (mostly non-automatable licensed/physical work incl. audiologists) and admin/marketing/billing roles (substantially automatable). Blended at 15% reflecting roughly one-quarter admin-type content at ~55% and three-quarters clinical-adjacent at ~2-5%.

pi_dist.rationale: Payers, not clients, set price: Medicare timed CPT units under an annually re-set conversion factor that was cut five consecutive years (-3.3% 2021, -0.8% 2022, -2.0% 2023, then $33.29->$32.35, -2.83% in 2025), MA prior-auth/visit caps, commercial per-visit case rates, workers'-comp fee schedules. The clinic keeps throughput gains from reclaimed documentation time inside a rate cycle (fee-for-service pays per visit, not per cost), but the rate-setting mechanism systematically transfers industry-wide efficiency to payers over successive cycles, and MA utilization management claws back volume. Referral flow is partly owned upstream by physicians/health systems; direct-access consumer marketing is a partial offset. Digital MSK platforms (Hinge, Sword) intermediate the employer channel for low-acuity visits.

pi_moat.rationale: State licensure of PT/OT/SLP/audiology is a hard barrier; care is physical and local (physical presence moat); referral relationships and payer contracts are clinic-level assets that largely survive acquisition. Clients cannot bypass the clinic with generic AI for hands-on care — the credible bypass is digital MSK (AI-guided home exercise) for the low-acuity chronic slice, sold through employers/payers, plus competition from physician-owned PT (621111) and hospital outpatient departments (622) for referrals. Patient switching costs are low, holding the value below 0.75.

t50_years.derivation: current_adoption_pct ~20% (mid-2026) with unusually steep momentum: AI scribes are cheap, require no workflow overhaul, and are distributed through the incumbent EMRs already installed in most clinics (WebPT, Net Health, Raintree all shipping AI documentation); SPRY reports 4% (Sep 2025) -> 45% (Feb 2026) of therapists on its platform using AI documentation; Confluent Health had 1,300+ providers live by Nov 2025 with a 3,100-provider rollout underway; APTA issued an ambient-scribe practice advisory Sep 2025 (mainstreaming signal). The EMR analog took ~7-8 years from ~20% penetration (2008) to majority (~2015-16), but EMR required replacing the entire documentation stack and had no channel — AI rides the existing channel. Discounting the vendor-biased SPRY curve, 30pp of remaining firm-level adoption at current trajectory ~= 3 years: t50 = 3.0.

current_adoption_pct.source: Triangulated estimate: SPRY press release (Yahoo Finance), APTA practice advisory, WebPT 2025 SORT press release, Net Health survey
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: SPRY: 'In September 2025, roughly 4% of therapists on SPRY were using AI documentation. By February 2026, that figure reached 45%'; Net Health survey (Dec 2024, n=200): 'Most respondents are somewhat positive (38%) or neutral (27%)' on AI, '80% of rehab therapy workers other than clinicians feel positive to very positive about AI/ambient documentation'; Confluent Health had 1,300+ of 3,100 providers live on AI documentation by Nov 2025; APTA published an AI-ambient-scribe practice advisory Sep 2025. No industry-wide firm-level adoption survey found — 20% is an estimate of firms materially using AI (defined as AI scribe/ambient documentation or AI front-office/RCM automation in routine production use), weighting large platforms (fast rollouts) against the ~25k sub-5-employee firms (slow).

historical_analogs.value: PT EMR adoption: ~7-8 years from ~20% penetration to majority. In 2008 ~80% of outpatient therapists documented on pen and paper (WebPT launch); adoption was pushed by the 2009 HITECH-era digitization wave and Medicare reporting requirements, reaching majority around 2015-16.
historical_analogs.source: SPRY EMR history blog (secondary republication of WebPT origin data); WebPT company history
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: 'In 2008, WebPT launched the first web-based EMR in the PT market at a time when 80% of outpatient therapists were documenting on pen and paper.'

owners_60plus_pct.source: Estimate triangulated from APTA workforce demographics (median PT age ~40; '17.1% of therapists will be over 65 within the next decade'), broker commentary that independent clinic owners average 50-60 with median owner age ~52 (CT Acquisitions), and Census ABS finding that over half of all US business owners are 55+.
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: Census: 'Over Half of U.S. Business Owners Were Age 55 and Over'; CT Acquisitions: 'median age around 52 for owners... independent physical therapy clinic owners average 50 to 60'. No direct 60+ owner-share statistic exists for this NAICS; 28% assumes owners skew ~10-12 years older than the young clinician base (median PT age ~40).

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: No documented succession shortage found. The profession is young (APTA: median PT age ~40) and the exit path is liquid — an active PE buyer pool (2,591 PE-affiliated clinics by 2024 per JAAOS) absorbs retiring owners; no APTA/PPS documentation of a succession gap located.

active_consolidators.source: JAAOS 'Trends in Private Equity Acquisition of US Physical Therapy Clinics, 2010 to 2024' + Provident Healthcare Partners / CT Acquisitions M&A coverage; named actives (last 24 months): Upstream Rehabilitation, Athletico, Confluent Health (incl. Access PT add-on Jan 2025), Ivy Rehab, ATI (2025 take-private), U.S. Physical Therapy (public consolidator; 50-clinic MSO deal Oct 2024, 8-clinic deal Jan 2026), CORA Health, PT Solutions, H2 Health, Alliance PTP, Empower PT, plus pediatric-therapy platforms.
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: 'By 2024, PE-affiliated physical therapy acquisitions accounted for 2,591 clinics in the US, with 91.1% of the deals comprising of add-on acquisitions'; 'Zero new platform buyouts were announced in 2024, but tuck-in activity resumed in 2025.'

buy_mult.source: CT Acquisitions Physical Therapy M&A Multiples Report 2026 (aggregating GF Data, Provident Healthcare Partners, Focus Investment Banking, IBBA Market Pulse)
buy_mult.figure_quoted: 'Sub-$500K SDE single-clinic: 3.0x to 5.0x SDE'; 'Multi-clinic $500K-$1M SDE: 4.5x to 6.5x SDE'; 'Lower Middle Market $1M-$3M EBITDA: 5.5x to 8.0x adjusted EBITDA'. 5.5x taken as the normalized-EBITDA median for small/entry targets: SDE-basis quotes convert down ~0.5-1.0x once market-rate owner-therapist compensation is deducted (rule 3), putting single/small clinics at ~4-5x EBITDA and the smallest true-EBITDA groups at the 5.5x band floor.

exit_mult.source: CT Acquisitions 2026 multiples report (PitchBook, Focus Investment Banking, Modern Healthcare) cross-checked against disclosed deals (Confluent/MOTION PT ~12.5x; USPH tuck-ins ~8-11x implied)
exit_mult.figure_quoted: 'Platform-Scale $10M+ EBITDA: 10.0x to 14.0x adjusted EBITDA (2024-2026), down from 12.0x to 15.0x observed during 2020-2022 peak'; 'MOTION PT's sale to Confluent Health was reported at ~12.5x'. 11.0x sits in the lower-middle of the current platform range, consistent with post-peak normalization.

pricing_structure.value: Effectively 100% fee-for-service billed as licensed-therapist time: Medicare Part B timed CPT units under the 8-minute rule at a fee schedule re-set annually (conversion factor cut five straight years through 2025); Medicare Advantage per-visit with prior-auth/visit caps; commercial per-visit case rates; workers'-comp state fee schedules; small but growing cash-pay/out-of-network segment (WebPT 2024 SORT: 'nearly 50% of respondents go out-of-network' for some volume). No retainer, cost-plus, or value-priced revenue of any significance.
pricing_structure.source: CMS CY2025 Medicare Physician Fee Schedule Final Rule; WebPT State of Rehab Therapy 2024
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: CY2025 final rule: conversion factor $32.35, a 2.83% cut from $33.29 in 2024 — fifth consecutive annual cut; PTA/OTA modifier services paid at 85%.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw 0.0685 = ~6.85pp of revenue freed at full capture — above the 3pp floor, and A = 3.33 < 5 anyway, so no inconsistency. On a ~15.8% margin base this is a material (roughly 40% relative) EBITDA uplift if captured, though pi_dist says payers claw back a large share over rate cycles.

terminal_value.justification: Answering the two mechanisms separately. (a) Payer rate-setting: real and pre-existing — five consecutive Medicare conversion-factor cuts (2021-2025) and the PTA/OTA 85% differential transfer efficiency to payers regardless of AI; AI throughput gains (documentation time reclaimed into billable visits) are kept by the operator within a rate cycle but eroded across cycles. This compresses capture (reflected in pi_dist=0.45), not demand. (b) Digital MSK substitution: Hinge/Sword evidence is strongest for surgery avoidance ('members undergo 67% fewer surgeries') and pain reduction, sold through employer/payer channels; independent evidence of wholesale in-clinic visit replacement is weak, exposure is confined to low-acuity chronic MSK, and Hinge itself now routes members into in-person care ('unifies digital and in-person care'). Mature AI does not deliver manual therapy, post-surgical rehab, pediatric speech, or audiology, and aging demographics grow the demand base. The operator's product survives mature AI; AI cuts a slice of the operator's admin cost rather than replacing the product. Demand survives = DURABLE, with margin capture, not existence, as the contested variable.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: Confirmed. V was built solely from labor_share, OEWS role mix, and per-role automatable fractions grounded in the billing-unit structure; A was derived solely from current adoption evidence (SPRY/Confluent/APTA/Net Health) and the PT-EMR historical analog. No deal activity, PE platform counts, or consolidation trends were used for V or A; they inform only B (CFD) and M (multiples).
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing mix is ~100% fee-for-service in payer-set units (Medicare timed CPT under the 8-minute rule, MA/commercial per-visit rates, workers'-comp schedules) with a small cash-pay tail. Because rates are set by payers, not negotiated per efficiency, AI savings do not flow to clients as lower fees within a cycle — but CMS conversion-factor resets and MA utilization management transfer industry-wide efficiency to payers over successive cycles, and prior-auth tightening can capture throughput gains as reduced authorized volume. This deflation channel is reflected in pi_dist = 0.45 (payer-owned pricing, partial clawback) rather than pi_moat, which reflects licensure/physical-presence barriers (0.65).

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A ranges 2.0 (t50=5) to 5.0 (t50=2) vs 3.33 at t50=3; S moves roughly 3.20-3.85 (S scales with A^0.2). This is the single largest score lever and rests on vendor-biased adoption curves (SPRY) plus an EMR analog with a different channel structure.
No industry-wide firm-level survey exists; feeds t50 directly — at 10% current adoption t50 stretches toward 4-5 (A~2-2.5), at 30% it compresses toward 2 (A=5).
OW ranges 0.286-0.80 vs 0.514; B ranges ~3.91-6.54; S moves roughly -0.21/+0.16 around 3.55. No direct owner-age statistic exists for this NAICS.
M = 4*(exit-buy)/buy ranges ~2.15 (6.5->10) to ~8.4 (4.5->14) vs 4.0; S moves roughly 3.13-4.12. Basis mixing (SDE vs adjusted EBITDA) at the small end is the main normalization risk.

reviewer_note: Heterogeneous code scored on the dominant sub-segment: outpatient PT clinics (PT+PTA ~33% of wage bill, plus most of the aide/front-office structure; OT/SLP clinics share the same time-billing economics). Audiology is structurally different (hearing-aid retail margin business, not a time business) but sits in the unlisted OEWS tail (~small share); pediatric speech (school/Medicaid contracts) also differs on payer mix. Billing-unit trap honored: licensed-clinical roles (~62% of wage bill) capped at 0-5% automatable — the honest V surface is the ~10-15% non-clinical wage share plus documentation-throughput slivers, yielding ai_replaceable_share 0.114; V=2.74 may still feel generous to skeptics of the remainder-bucket blend (0.15 on a 24.5% opaque tail is the softest assumption in V). Formula-vs-intuition: no overrides; all scores are mechanical. Dataset caveats used as given: labor_share has a 2024-wages/2022-receipts vintage mismatch; n_band=821 consists entirely of 100-499 and 500+ employee firms, i.e. the $1-10M EBITDA 'band targets' here are already multi-clinic groups, not single sites — buy_mult for actual band-size targets is therefore closer to the 5.5-8.0x tier than to single-clinic SDE deals, compressing realizable M below the headline single-site arbitrage; n_band margin uses the Damodaran Hospitals/Healthcare Facilities 15.8% bucket (ESTIMATE). Exit environment note: platform multiples are off their 2020-22 peak (ATI's distressed 2025 take-private; Confluent's called-off 2024 sale), so exit_mult=11.0 carries real downside risk toward 10x. Terminal class DURABLE but with a payer-capture asterisk: C=2.93 is the binding constraint on S, which is the economically correct signature for a payer-rate-set, time-billed industry.
